voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: It uses smell to find ripe fruit and to identify individual group members.
Its loud calls carry far through the forest and it listens for responses.
Its eyes face forward for precise depth perception while leaping between trees.
It sniffs branches to read scent marks left by other lemurs.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in the mature rainforest canopy of eastern Madagascar.
It forages mainly in the upper levels of tall, fruit-bearing trees.
Dense forest provides escape routes from aerial and ground predators.
A large home range is needed because fruiting trees are scattered across the forest.
How does it send messages?: Its call is one of the loudest produced by any primate.
This call warns the group of predators and tells neighbors a territory is occupied.
Males scent-mark by lowering their chin and chest onto a branch.
Females mark territory by rubbing the anogenital region on horizontal surfaces.
What does it do when something changes?: It gives loud alarm calls immediately when it detects a predator.
After an alarm, the group moves into denser foliage and becomes quieter.
Scent marking increases during the breeding season to communicate readiness.
It avoids areas of the forest that have been disturbed or cleared.
How does it learn?: Infants stay in nests early in life and gradually follow adults into the canopy.
Young lemurs learn fruiting tree locations by traveling with adult group members.
They learn scent-marking behavior by watching adults from about five months of age.
How does it have babies?: The female builds up to fifteen nests before giving birth to her young.
Two to six infants are born after about 102 days of gestation.
Infants cannot cling and remain in the nest for two to three weeks.
Related and unrelated group members help guard nests while the mother forages.
What are its limits?: Heavy deforestation in Madagascar has removed most of its forest habitat.
Small, isolated forest patches cannot support groups that need large fruiting ranges.
Even its faster reproduction rate cannot offset the pace of habitat loss.
It is Critically Endangered due to ongoing deforestation and hunting.
What does this teach us?: Building fifteen nests before birth protects young from canopy dangers.
Shared nest-guarding across the group helps more infants survive.
Its calls carry farther through the forest than any other lemur's.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Predominantly frugivorous, dependent on ripe fruit from large-crowned rainforest trees.
Uses loud, coordinated barking calls to warn the group of predators.
Builds nests in the upper trees for infants (unusual compared to many other primates).
Lives in Madagascar's lowland rainforests and is severely affected by deforestation.
still_debated: How stable long-term social groupings are outside of feeding congregations.
Exact role of scent marking in mediating between different female lineages.
How much individual recognition is carried in specific call variations.
common_failure_modes: Lumping behavior with red ruffed or ring-tailed lemurs (different species, different ecology).
Assuming zoo group structure matches wild group structure.
Treating the barking alarm as 'language' rather than a context-specific signal.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not extrapolate from other lemur species without naming the source species.
Do not describe as 'nocturnal' — this species is day-active.
Do not state precise troop sizes without flagging as 'varies widely by site.'
